deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding the student through questions rather than providing direct answers—i.e., it never articulates that this approach supports active reasoning, retention, or skill development. The tutoring constraints are enforced but the rationale is never stated.

In Transcript 2 (Turn 10) and Transcript 3 (Turn 10), students make clear progress—correctly distinguishing stressors or building a solid justification path—but the tutor moves immediately to the next question without prompting any reflection on what changed in their thinking or why the improved reasoning worked.

In Transcript 1, the student repeatedly asks for the same conceptual clarification (stressor vs. sector vs. impact, big-picture logic) across nearly every turn, and the tutor cycles through similar explanations and re-explanations without meaningfully consolidating, reframing at a different level, or suggesting a different approach to break the loop. The student remains stuck in the same confusion pattern through Turn 10 without the tutor switching strategy effectively.

grade.overview: The tutor performs well across Transcripts 2 and 3, maintaining Socratic guidance, building on student progress, and keeping responses focused and appropriately sized. Transcript 1 reveals a significant weakness: the student is deeply stuck in a conceptual loop and the tutor repeats similar explanations and question types across 10 turns without switching strategy, consolidating, or reframing at a different level—triggering the 2.1 redundancy deduction. Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explains why it is guiding rather than answering directly (no meta-learning rationale), and misses opportunities to prompt reflection after student breakthroughs in Transcripts 2 and 3.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps key idea generation student-led across all three transcripts. 1.2 is fully intact: the tutor builds on student progress and does not respond to the wrong confusion point in any systematic way. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutoring rationale is never articulated (1.3.A.a), and after clear student improvement in Transcripts 2 and 3, the tutor moves on without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a). 2.1 loses all 4 points due to Transcript 1, where the student cycles through the same conceptual confusion for 10 turns and the tutor repeats similar explanatory moves without consolidating, reframing at a new level, or suggesting a different approach. 2.2 is fully intact across all transcripts. Communication quality (3.1, 3.2) is strong throughout: responses are bite-sized, tone is supportive, and no grading language appears.
